structured_choices.data_source: Historical delivery logs from a single urban logistics operator spanning 12–18 months. Data includes: per-delivery timestamps, origin/destination GPS coordinates, customer location type (residential/commercial), time-of-day, day-of-week, weather conditions (temperature, precipitation, visibility), traffic incident reports (accidents, road closures), vehicle type, driver ID (anonymized), and actual delivery completion time. External data: historical traffic speed profiles by road segment and time-of-day; weather archives; public event calendars (holidays, major events, sports games). Data collection period should span multiple seasonal cycles to capture variation in traffic patterns and customer behavior.
structured_choices.label_definition: For the heuristic baseline: label is the actual delivery time achieved by the incumbent routing algorithm on each historical route. For the RL model: the reward signal is defined as negative delivery time (in minutes) plus a penalty term for customer satisfaction impact (−5 points per unit drop in satisfaction rating below 4.5/5). The RL agent learns a policy that minimizes this combined cost. For comparative evaluation, the primary outcome is average delivery time (minutes) on held-out test routes, with customer satisfaction as a secondary outcome constraint.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with stratification by season and geography. Divide the 12–18 month dataset into three non-overlapping windows: (1) Months 1–9: training data for both heuristic and RL agent. (2) Months 10–12: validation data for RL policy refinement and hyperparameter tuning. (3) Months 13–18: held-out test set, never touched during development. This temporal structure prevents the RL agent from learning future traffic patterns and ensures evaluation reflects real-world deployment. Within each split, stratify by (a) season (winter/spring/summer/fall) and (b) urban zone (downtown/residential/industrial) to ensure all splits contain representative distributions. This guards against seasonal confounds and geographic biases in the test set.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: Fit all preprocessing transformers (scalers for GPS distance, traffic speed, weather features; encoders for categorical features like day-of-week, weather condition; imputation strategies) on the training data (months 1–9) only. Apply these fitted transformers to validation and test sets without refitting. This prevents data leakage from test statistics influencing the model. For the heuristic baseline, use the same preprocessed feature representations to ensure fair comparison.
structured_choices.features: Raw features: (1) Route geometry: Euclidean distance, road-network distance (via map API), estimated free-flow time. (2) Temporal: hour-of-day (cyclic encoding), day-of-week, is_holiday flag, season. (3) Traffic: historical average speed for the route segment at the requested time-of-day, recent traffic incidents (count, type) within 500m of route, congestion index (0–1 scale from traffic data). (4) Weather: temperature, precipitation, visibility, wind speed (all interpolated to delivery start time and location). (5) Delivery context: vehicle type, customer location type (residential/commercial), package weight category (if available), driver experience level (years, binned). Derived features: (1) time-of-day congestion multiplier (ratio of current speed to free-flow speed). (2) route complexity score (number of turns, road type diversity). (3) weather severity index (composite of temp extremes, precipitation, visibility). No leakage: all features are known at the time routing decisions are made; no future information is encoded.
structured_choices.model: Deep Q-Network (DQN) agent with experience replay and target network. State space: (route_distance, current_congestion, weather_index, time_of_day, day_of_week, vehicle_type, driver_experience). Action space: discrete set of 10–20 candidate routes (generated by a fast heuristic route generator for computational tractability; RL agent ranks/selects among them). Reward: −delivery_time − 5 × max(0, 4.5 − customer_satisfaction). Architecture: 3-layer fully connected neural network (128–256 hidden units per layer, ReLU activation). Train with Adam optimizer, learning rate 0.001, replay buffer size 10,000, target network update every 500 steps. Epsilon-greedy exploration (ε decaying from 0.1 to 0.01 over training). Training proceeds over 50–100 episodes, each episode comprising 500–1000 delivery decisions (sampled from training dataset or simulated rollouts).
structured_choices.baseline: Heuristic baseline: the incumbent routing algorithm currently in production. This algorithm is assumed to use distance + time estimates and simple greedy logic (e.g., nearest-neighbor, shortest estimated time). Equivalent tuning effort: the heuristic will be evaluated in its standard production configuration; no hyperparameter search is applied to the heuristic (because it has no learnable parameters). However, to ensure the baseline is not strawmanned, verify it uses the same preprocessed features and is applied consistently across all test routes. The heuristic must be given the same external traffic/weather information available to the RL agent. Justification: the heuristic represents the current operational status quo; beating it demonstrates practical value. The comparison is fair because both methods operate on the same feature set and are evaluated under identical conditions.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Average delivery time (minutes) on the test set. Justification: the operational objective is to reduce delivery times to lower costs and improve service reliability. This metric directly aligns with stakeholder goals. It is not subject to class imbalance (regression metric) and is interpretable in business terms. Report as mean ± 95% CI (computed via bootstrap). Secondary threshold: the RL model must not degrade customer satisfaction (test set mean satisfaction ≥ incumbent baseline mean satisfaction). If satisfaction drops, the test is failed regardless of time improvement.
secondary_metrics: Customer satisfaction rating (mean ± CI on test set)
Delivery time percentile distribution (p50, p75, p95) to assess tail performance and consistency
Success rate: % of deliveries completed on time (if SLA target available)
Cost per delivery (estimated as delivery_time × driver_wage_rate + vehicle_operating_cost)
Robustness score: average delivery time improvement measured separately within each season (winter, spring, summer, fall) to demonstrate consistent gains across seasonal variation
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning uses the validation set (months 10–12). During validation, the RL agent is trained on the training set and its policy is evaluated on validation deliveries every 10 episodes. Select the policy (checkpoint) that achieves the lowest average delivery time on the validation set (while maintaining satisfaction ≥ baseline). The heuristic baseline is not tuned; it is evaluated as-is. The final test set (months 13–18) is reserved for a single evaluation pass at the end. No re-tuning or model selection occurs on the test set.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is locked and never touched until final evaluation. After model selection (via validation set), the selected RL policy is deployed on test routes exactly once. Compute primary and secondary metrics on this single pass. Report the results and do not re-optimize, re-tune, or adjust the model based on test performance. This ensures an unbiased estimate of generalization performance.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Traffic variability: controlled by temporal stratification (seasons, months) and by including traffic features (historical speed profiles, incident reports) in the feature set. Weather: included as explicit features and stratification ensures each split contains diverse weather conditions. Road closures and unexpected events: modeled as traffic incidents in the feature set; the RL agent should learn to route around them. Geographic bias: stratify all splits by urban zone (downtown/residential/industrial) to ensure test set contains representative geographies. Driver and vehicle differences: include driver experience and vehicle type as features so the model/heuristic can condition on these. Spillover effects (e.g., one delivery blocking another): not directly modeled, but if significant, would appear as higher variance in test metrics — report variance carefully. Data quality: manually audit 2% of delivered packages (log accuracy check) and flag any systematic errors; exclude corrupted delivery logs (missing GPS, implausible timestamps). Seasonal drift: report results separately for each season in the test set to verify the RL agent generalizes across seasonal conditions. External validity: this experiment establishes performance on a single urban logistics operator in a specific geography; results may not generalize to other cities, operators, or climates. Explicitly scope the claim to the test domain.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Deep Q-Learning vs. Heuristic Routing for Last-Mile Delivery

### 1. Data Collection and Preparation

The experiment uses 12–18 months of delivery logs from a single urban logistics operator. Data includes per-delivery timestamps, GPS coordinates for origin and destination, vehicle type, driver ID, weather conditions at delivery time, traffic incident reports, and actual delivery completion time and customer satisfaction rating. External data sources include historical traffic speed profiles (by road segment and hour-of-day) and public event calendars (holidays, major events). Collect multiple seasonal cycles to capture traffic and behavior variation.

**Data quality:** Audit 2% of logs for accuracy (verify GPS plausibility, check timestamp consistency). Exclude any delivery records with missing critical fields (GPS, timestamp, completion time). Document any systematic errors (e.g., GPS dropout in certain zones) and report as a limitation.

### 2. Temporal and Stratified Split Strategy

Divide the dataset into three non-overlapping, temporally ordered windows:
- **Training (Months 1–9):** Used to train both the RL agent and for final heuristic evaluation.
- **Validation (Months 10–12):** Used for RL hyperparameter tuning and policy selection only.
- **Test (Months 13–18):** Held-out set, locked until final evaluation.

Within each split, stratify by (1) season (winter, spring, summer, fall) and (2) urban zone (downtown, residential, industrial) to ensure every split contains representative distributions. This temporal ordering prevents the RL agent from "learning" future traffic patterns (temporal leakage), and stratification guards against seasonal and geographic bias in the test set.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Construct features from raw delivery data:
- **Route geometry:** Euclidean distance, road-network distance, free-flow travel time.
- **Temporal:** Hour-of-day (cyclic encoding), day-of-week, holiday flag, season.
- **Traffic:** Historical average speed for route at requested time, traffic incident count/type within 500m, congestion index (current speed / free-flow speed).
- **Weather:** Temperature, precipitation, visibility, wind speed (interpolated to delivery start location and time).
- **Delivery context:** Vehicle type, customer location type (residential/commercial), driver experience level (binned years).
- **Derived:** Time-of-day congestion multiplier, route complexity score (turn count, road type diversity), weather severity index.

**Fit all transformers (scalers, encoders, imputation) on the training set only.** Apply fitted transformers to validation and test sets without refitting. This prevents test data statistics from influencing preprocessing parameters (data leakage). All features are known at routing decision time; no future information is encoded.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**RL Model:** Deep Q-Network (DQN) agent with experience replay and target network. State: (route_distance, congestion, weather_index, time_of_day, day_of_week, vehicle_type, driver_experience). Action: select from 10–20 candidate routes (generated by a fast heuristic route generator). Reward: −delivery_time (minutes) − 5 × max(0, 4.5 − customer_satisfaction_rating), penalizing both longer deliveries and satisfaction drops. Neural network: 3 fully connected layers (128–256 hidden units, ReLU), trained with Adam (lr=0.001), replay buffer 10k, target network update every 500 steps. ε-greedy exploration (ε: 0.1 → 0.01). Train for 50–100 episodes.

**Baseline:** The incumbent heuristic routing algorithm currently in production. Assume it uses distance + time estimates with simple logic (e.g., nearest-neighbor, shortest estimated time). The heuristic receives the same preprocessed features and traffic/weather information as the RL agent. No hyperparameter tuning is applied to the heuristic (it has no learnable parameters), but verify it is evaluated consistently across all test routes. Justification: the heuristic is the operational status quo; beating it demonstrates practical value. Both methods are evaluated on the same feature set and identical conditions, ensuring a fair comparison.

### 5. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning uses the validation set (months 10–12). During training on the training set, evaluate the RL policy on validation routes every 10 episodes. Select the checkpoint that achieves the lowest average delivery time on the validation set while maintaining customer satisfaction ≥ baseline (no satisfaction regression). The heuristic is not tuned and is evaluated in its production configuration.

### 6. Test Set Evaluation

After model selection via validation, deploy the selected RL policy on the test set (months 13–18) exactly once. The test set is not touched before this final pass and not re-optimized afterward. Report all metrics from this single pass.

### 7. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Average delivery time (minutes) on the test set, reported as mean ± 95% CI (bootstrap). This directly aligns with the operational objective to reduce delivery time and lower costs.

**Constraint:** Mean customer satisfaction on test set must be ≥ baseline mean. If satisfaction drops below baseline, the experiment fails regardless of time improvement.

**Secondary metrics:** (1) Customer satisfaction mean ± CI. (2) Delivery time distribution (p50, p75, p95). (3) % on-time delivery (if SLA available). (4) Estimated cost per delivery. (5) Robustness: average delivery time improvement computed separately for each season to verify generalization across seasonal variation.

### 8. Confound Management

Traffic variability and weather: controlled via temporal stratification and explicit inclusion as features. Road closures and unexpected events: modeled as traffic incident features. Geographic bias: stratified by urban zone in all splits. Driver/vehicle differences: included as conditional features. Seasonal drift: report test results separately by season to verify consistency. Data quality issues: exclude corrupted logs and audit for systematic errors.

**Scope:** This experiment establishes performance on a single urban operator in a specific geography and climate. Generalization to other operators, cities, or climates is not established by this design and should be stated explicitly as out-of-scope.
